title: Agora: Toward Autonomous Bug Detection in Production-Level Consensus Protocols with LLM Agents
abstract: Consensus protocols form the backbone of distributed systems and blockchains, where implementation bugs can cause data corruption and financial losses. While LLM-based approaches show promise in code analysis, they struggle with deep protocol-level logic bugs involving complex state-dependent behaviors across multiple execution stages. We present Agora, a domain-aware multi-agent framework that integrates hypothesis-driven testing with LLM capabilities for systematic protocol verification. Agora employs specialized agents that collaboratively explore protocol state spaces, synthesize attack scenarios using domain-specific constraints, and validate findings through iterative refinement. This explicit role separation enables reasoning about global protocol invariants beyond single-function code analysis. We evaluate Agora on four consensus implementations (Raft, EPaxos, HotStuff, BullShark) using four state-of-the-art LLMs. Agora discovers 15 previously unknown protocol-level logic bugs that violate safety properties, while existing LLM-based agents fail to detect any such protocol-level logic bugs. Our results demonstrate that domain-aware multi-agent collaboration is essential for detecting deep logic bugs in complex protocols.
decision_comment: This paper proposes an agent-based technique for identifying bugs in BFT and CFT consensus protocols. The reviewers appreciated that the work is tackling a real-world problem and appears to be quite effective, identifying over 15 unknown bugs in real consensus protocols. The reviewers made a number of requests regarding strengthening the evaluation, including a comparison to non-LLM based approaches; during discussion, we ultimately concluded that for this relatively less-mature class of protocols and implementations, there is not a natural baseline that the authors should have compared against. That being said, we encourage the authors to discuss the feedback from the author-reviewer discussion in their revision. The reviewers also felt that the ML innovations of this work are modest, but the application to an important software engineering and distributed systems problem is valuable and novel. Moreover, the direct outputs of this paper (namely, the identified bugs) could be quite useful to the distributed systems community. Hence, overall, we remain positive about the overall significance of the work.

reviews.summary: This paper proposed a novel LLM-based detection of logic errors of existing BFT or CFT protocosl. Their system is composed of three “domain”-aware agents which deal wih specific roles during Agora execution. They found 15 new bugs among BFT-based and CFT-based  protocols.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strength:

1. Previously unknown 15 logic bugs were identified by Agora

2. Showed that employing multiple domain-aware agents turned out to effective

3. AGORA deals with both CFT and BFT uniformly.

Weakness:

1. There is no comparison with non-AI-based consensus protocol analysis techniques, even no references. 

2. As to those bugs Agora found in Appendix, it is not clear if they cannot be easily detectable by human, and if so, it would be better to introduce them in the main text, emphasize the cleverness of  Agora, and explain how Agora could detect them, possibly showing the problematic source code lines. Generally, the main text of the paper includes somewhat abstract explanations only, without any real cases or attack scenarios, so it is not clear if Agora really found some subtle, hard logic bugs that human could not think. 

3. There is no mention on why they chose HotStuff와 BullShark, rather than some mainstream chains such as Bitcoin, Ethereum, Solana, Avalanche, etc. If Agora found a bug in actively used main chains, then it would be a big achievement.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
(1) When defects of a blockchain protocol is detected and reported, the blockchain foundation often gives some grants, which is boasted in the security research papers. What about AGORA which found more than 15 bugs?

(2) As an example of a nonAI-based bug detection technique, there is a paper, "LOKI: State-Aware Fuzzing Framework for the Implementation of Blockchain Consensus Protocols (NDSS ‘23)", and I wonder how AGORA differs from LOKI. Even if precise evaluation cannot be performed, these techniques would better be mentioned and compared against. 

(3) Have you tried AGORA with Ethereum or Bitcoin protocols? Why choosing HotStuff와 BullShark?

(4) Would it be possible to move some concrete examples of defects, how to make the attack scenarios, or some successful collaboration cases among agents, to the main text to make the paper more realistic?  In my own reading, the current main text includes somewhat abstract concepts, so I could not easily see any new insight that this approach can provide. So, I wonder if you can explain the insights more explicitly with examples. Finding 15 new bugs is excellent, but the process of how AGORA found them was not vivid nor well explained.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes Agora, an automated vulnerability detection framework designed to use an LLM-based multi-agent system to discover logical errors in implementations of distributed consensus protocols. The system consists of three agents—Orchestrator, Strategy, and TestGen—which together construct a hypothesis-driven testing workflow by combining a domain knowledge base, attack scenario generation, and a test generation and reflection loop to explore potential protocol vulnerabilities. The authors evaluate the system on implementations of four consensus protocols—Raft, EPaxos, HotStuff, and BullShark—and report discovering 15 previously unknown protocol-level logical errors. Experimental results suggest that, compared with simple LLM-agent baseline methods, Agora demonstrates stronger capability in discovering complex protocol vulnerabilities.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
The research problem has practical significance. Automated detection of logical vulnerabilities in distributed consensus protocol implementations is an important and practically relevant problem.
The system design is relatively clear. The paper organizes the task through a multi-agent architecture, separating strategy generation, test generation, and execution/validation modules, and the overall workflow is described in a structured manner.
Evaluation on real-world systems. Experiments are conducted on multiple real protocol implementations, and the paper provides a certain level of case studies and ablation analysis.

Weaknesses:
Fairness of the baseline comparison and attribution of contributions are questionable.
The paper uses ReAct + code-reading tools as the main baseline. However, ReAct is a general reasoning framework and does not include the key domain knowledge relied upon by Agora (e.g., the error-pattern library and protocol constraints). As a result, the experiments essentially compare a system equipped with substantial manually curated domain knowledge with a general LLM framework lacking such knowledge. Under this setup, it is expected that the baseline fails to discover complex vulnerabilities. The results therefore mainly demonstrate the importance of domain knowledge, rather than convincingly establishing the advantage of the multi-agent architecture itself.
A more appropriate comparison would provide the same domain knowledge to a single-agent system (e.g., through prompting or retrieval augmentation) and then compare it with Agora. Otherwise, it is difficult to determine whether the performance gains come from the multi-agent design or from the injected domain knowledge.
The definition and validation of “protocol-level logical errors” are not sufficiently rigorous.
The paper claims to have discovered 15 zero-day vulnerabilities and protocol-level logical errors, but it does not provide a clear criterion for distinguishing protocol-level logical errors from implementation bugs. Examples in the appendix suggest that both types of issues essentially manifest as implementation behavior deviating from protocol expectations; some lead to liveness violations while others lead to safety violations, but the paper mainly distinguishes them through simple categorization. In addition, the paper does not clarify whether these vulnerabilities have been reported to project maintainers or independently confirmed. Therefore, the central claim of discovering 15 protocol-level zero-day vulnerabilities lacks sufficient rigor in both definition and validation.
Limitations in experimental design and evaluation setup.
The experiments use a fixed 3-hour runtime budget per run. For protocol testing tasks with extremely large exploration spaces, such a time-based budget may introduce unfairness across methods. In particular, baseline approaches without prior domain knowledge may require longer exploration time. A budget defined in terms of API calls or number of generated tests might be more appropriate.
Moreover, experiments cover only four protocol implementations, which is still relatively small in scale. The paper also lacks comparisons with existing protocol testing or fuzzing tools, making it difficult to clearly understand the system’s practical advantage over prior methods.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
Have the authors tried a single-agent version (e.g., providing the same domain knowledge base to a single LLM) as a fairer baseline?

Could the error patterns in the knowledge base originate from the code repositories used in the experiments or from their historical issues? If so, how do the authors mitigate potential data leakage concerns?

What is the rationale for selecting 3 hours as the experimental budget? Have the authors considered alternative budget definitions, such as a fixed number of API calls?

reviews.summary: The paper develops an LLM-based framework for detecting logical errors in implementations of consensus protocols. The main contribution is an architecture consisting of multiple agents, and division of subtasks across different modules. The framework is evaluated on real-world implementations drawn from GitHub, and the framework is able to find previously unreported logical errors.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
+ The overall result in terms of finding logical errors in real-world implementations of a key protocol is impressive.
+ The design of the Agora system is well motivated by comparing it what existing LLMs and static analysis tools can do in terms of bug detection.
+ Experimental evaluation is extensive: ablation studies support the inclusion of different steps in Agora; bugs found are explained etc

Weakness:
- The system is specialized for one particular, though important, distributed protocol
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
1. where did the 5 classes logical bugs described on page 3 come from?
2. can you explain what a "constraint" is. I had assumed constraints to be something formal (in logic) to be checked by constraint solvers, but apparently not. the appendix gives some examples, but doesn't explain what it is
3. did you try to use a model checker (or similar analysis tool) to find the reported bugs?
4. would the same method work for, say, network routing protocols? how much effort/specialization would be required?

reviews.summary: This paper introduces Agora, a multi-agent framework for detecting deep bugs in consensus protocols. The key idea is to combine hypothesis-driven testing (HDT) with LLM-based agents that are aware of domain-specific constraints. The framework employs three specialized agents to decouple workflow control, explore protocol state spaces, and generate test scenarios. Evaluated on four consensus implementations using four LLMs, Agora discovered 15 previously unknown safety-critical bugs, whereas existing LLM-based agents found none.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

1. It is worth noting that the problem addressed in this paper—bugs in consensus protocols—is highly important and carries real-world impact.

2. One strength of this work is that the ablation studies demonstrate the significant contribution of each proposed component, confirming their effectiveness in the overall framework.

Weaknesses:

1. Presentation: Line 81 introduces the use of hypothesis-driven testing (HDT). However, the rationale behind choosing HDT over other testing approaches is not immediately clear. It would strengthen the paper to elaborate on why HDT is particularly suitable for detecting bugs in consensus protocols and what motivated its adoption in this context.

2. Originality: It would be helpful to further emphasize the technical challenges addressed in this work. For example, regarding the claim of "combining domain knowledge of distributed consensus with LLM-based agents, " what specific challenges did this integration entail? Similarly, for the statement about "effectively following the hypothesis-driven testing (HDT) paradigm and employing three agents, " it would strengthen the paper to explicitly discuss the difficulties overcome in implementing this multi-agent approach. Highlighting these challenges would better underscore the novelty and impact of the proposed system.

3. The current definition and categorization of protocol-level logic bugs appear somewhat unconvincing. It is unclear whether this classification follows established conventions in the field or is derived from the authors' own experience. Several specific points warrant clarification: (1) What exactly constitutes a "simple logical flaw" as mentioned in line 163, and how does it differ from protocol-level logic bugs? (2) Do the five proposed categories of protocol-level logic bugs comprehensively cover all possible types, or might there be omissions?

4. While the authors claim to have analyzed and summarized bug patterns, it remains unclear whether the insights derived from 34 known bugs are sufficiently comprehensive or representative. Moreover, the statement in line 253 that this analysis provides "a succinct knowledge library for Agora that only offers the necessary domain knowledge" raises further questions: it is unclear how the information extracted from these 34 cases can be validated as constituting the "necessary" domain knowledge, rather than being incomplete or potentially biased. Clarifying the rationale behind this claim or discussing its limitations would enhance the rigor of the work.

Other comments:

1. Line 89: The term "consensus scenarios properties" appears to refer to a static notion, whereas the parallel structure with "workflow control" and "test generation" implies that an action or process is intended. I suggest replacing it with "bug scenario generation".

2. I notice that in Table 1, the number of detected implementation bugs is presented as "fewer is better." Even if the proposed method is not designed for detecting such bugs, it is not immediately clear why detecting them would be considered a negative outcome. The rationale behind this evaluation criterion would benefit from clarification.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
1. Please highlight the key technical challenges addressed, as this would help readers better appreciate the originality of the work.

2. Could the analysis based on 34 known bugs yield incomplete or biased domain knowledge, and if so, how might this affect the proposed approach?

3. Why HDT is particularly suitable for detecting bugs in consensus protocols and what motivated its adoption in this context？
